story.headline: US–Iran ceasefire collapses: Washington resumes strikes and Hormuz blockade, Iran hits Gulf bases
story.summary: CENTCOM resumed strikes on Iran and reimposed a naval blockade of Iranian ports after the MoU collapsed; Iran retaliated overnight against US assets in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan, with 260+ injured and 30+ civilians killed in Iran.
story.display_body: US Central Command (CENTCOM — the US military command for the Middle East) said it launched a fresh seven-hour wave of strikes from about 02:00 GMT Wednesday, hitting "dozens of military targets" — missile and drone sites, naval capabilities and coastal defences — near the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow chokepoint through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil passes. Iran's health ministry said more than 260 people were injured and over 30 civilians killed in the latest US attacks, with strikes reported on the port city of Chabahar and a base at Bampur in the southeast. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it struck US military facilities in Bahrain (the home port of the US Fifth Fleet), a logistics centre at Mina Abdullah in Kuwait, and the al-Azraq base in Jordan; Kuwait reported a naval vessel hit and four personnel wounded, and Jordan said it downed four Iranian ballistic missiles. Tehran declared the memorandum of understanding (MoU — the tentative peace framework signed less than a month ago) void, and the IRGC threatened to halt all Middle East energy exports, warning that oil and gas would flow "for everyone or for no one."
story.why: A signed de-escalation framework has unravelled in under a month into direct US strikes on Iranian soil and Iranian attacks on three Gulf states at once — the widest regional spillover yet, with an explicit threat to global oil supply through Hormuz.

story.headline: Russia pounds Odesa ports for a fifth day; Ukraine strikes 20 Russian Black Sea vessels
story.summary: A massive Russian drone-and-missile attack on Odesa killed at least three, while Ukraine said it hit 17 Russian oil tankers and other vessels in the Black Sea.
story.display_body: Odesa region governor Oleh Kiper described a "massive" overnight drone-and-missile attack on 14–15 July on civilian, industrial and port infrastructure; three were killed and three wounded in Odesa, with further strikes on the port of Chornomorsk. Russia's defence ministry confirmed hitting the Odesa and Chornomorsk ports, saying it targeted fuel-storage and drone-assembly sites. Kyiv's drone-force commander said Ukraine in turn hit 17 Russian oil tankers, two gas tankers and a tugboat, part of a campaign to disrupt Russian logistics and isolate occupied Crimea.
story.why: The fighting has concentrated on the Greater Odesa ports that handle most of Ukraine's grain and cargo exports, so the escalation threatens both civilian lives and the wartime economy — and pulls Black Sea shipping lanes further into the war.

story.headline: Federal Court upholds Valais ban on disposable e-cigarettes
story.summary: Switzerland's Federal Court dismissed the industry's appeal against the canton of Valais's sales ban on single-use e-cigarettes, ruling cantons may act while the Federal Council has not.
story.display_body: The court held that the cantonal ban is compatible with federal law and is not a disproportionate infringement of economic freedom. Its reasoning turns on a gap in federal regulation: the Environmental Protection Act (USG) gives the Federal Council the power to ban the marketing of single-use products, but — the court noted — it "has not to date exercised that competence" for disposable e-cigarettes, so cantons are currently free to enact such bans themselves.
story.why: The ruling confirms that Swiss cantons can regulate disposable vapes ahead of the Confederation, giving Valais's ban legal footing and a template other cantons can follow while federal rules lag.

story.headline: Federal Council's Lex Koller tightening emerges weakened as consultation closes
story.summary: The public consultation on the Federal Council's plan to further restrict property purchases by foreigners closed on 15 July amid business-sector opposition.
story.display_body: The draft would make non-EU/EFTA nationals (i.e. from outside the European Union and the European Free Trade Association, of which Switzerland is a member) seek authorisation to buy homes, further limit holiday-home purchases, and bar foreign-domiciled buyers from acquiring shares in listed residential-property companies and regularly traded real-estate funds. The proposal splits the political spectrum: parties at both ends frame it as ending an abuse, while business-aligned groups warn of serious economic damage, and the mountain-regions association (SAB) opposes halving the quotas for holiday-home purchases, arguing it would hurt Alpine areas without easing the housing shortage.
story.why: Lex Koller shapes who can buy Swiss property, so a tightening — even a diluted one — would directly affect the cost and availability of homes and the flow of foreign capital into Swiss real estate.

story.headline: Richemont sales jump 20% in first quarter, led by jewellery
story.summary: Geneva-based luxury group Richemont posted a 20% constant-rate rise in Q1 sales to end-June, with Jewellery Maisons up 24%.
story.display_body: The Jewellery Maisons (Cartier, Van Cleef & Arpels, Buccellati, Vhernier) rose 24% — a seventh straight quarter of double-digit growth — while Specialist Watchmakers gained 8% and the Fashion & Accessories division 9%. Growth was broad across regions, led by Japan (+36%) and the Americas (+27%), with Europe up 11%; the group's net cash position reached €9.1 billion.
story.why: Richemont is a bellwether for the Swiss luxury-goods sector and a major SMI-listed employer, and the strong beat signals resilient high-end demand despite what the company called a "volatile" macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop.

story.headline: A cryogenic strontium tweezer array holds single atoms for two hours
story.summary: A cryogenically-cooled neutral-atom apparatus traps single strontium-88 atoms in optical tweezers for up to two hours while keeping full optical access.
story.display_body: Neutral-atom quantum computers trap individual atoms in optical tweezers — tightly focused laser beams that hold one atom each — and are scaling toward tens of thousands of qubits. The bottleneck is a trade-off: keeping atoms trapped for a long time usually means sealing them inside a cold, cramped enclosure that blocks the optical access the machine needs to manipulate them. This group reports a cryogenically-cooled apparatus that sidesteps the trade-off, holding single strontium-88 atoms in a tweezer array for vacuum-limited lifetimes of up to two hours while preserving full optical access, and without a complex cryogenic shroud. Room-temperature setups typically lose atoms in tens of seconds; a two-hour lifetime means an atom essentially never leaks away during a computation.
story.why: Longer trap lifetimes translate directly into more qubits held stably at once, so this is a concrete engineering step toward the large atom arrays that error-corrected quantum computing will need.
affiliations: Max Planck Institute of Quantum Optics

story.headline: Infinite-layer nickelates may host a mixed s+id superconducting state
story.summary: Theory combining first-principles and Eliashberg calculations argues infinite-layer nickelates realize an s+id superconducting state from spin fluctuations plus electron-phonon coupling, explaining patchy tunneling data.
story.display_body: Nickelates — nickel-oxide compounds chemically tuned to mimic the cuprate superconductors — are a leading testbed for unconventional superconductivity, where electrons pair up in ways ordinary theory cannot explain. Recent tunneling experiments saw the symmetry of the electron pairing vary from spot to spot on the same sample, which nobody could explain. Combining first-principles calculations with Eliashberg pairing theory, the authors argue two mechanisms coexist: magnetic spin fluctuations produce a d-wave state on the nickel orbital, while electron-phonon coupling produces an s-wave state on a separate interstitial orbital, yielding a combined s+id state. The s component only appears in a narrow carrier-density window, so local oxygen defects would tune it up or down — a natural explanation for the patchy tunneling data.
story.why: It offers a testable microscopic story for a puzzling experiment, and a mixed s+id state, if confirmed, would be a rare form of pairing that breaks time-reversal symmetry.

story.headline: A fresh independent estimate of the muon g-2's thorniest term
story.summary: A continuum-QCD (Dyson-Schwinger/Bethe-Salpeter) calculation of the leading hadronic-vacuum-polarization contribution to the muon g-2 lands at 709.7e-10, agreeing with lattice results.
story.display_body: The muon's anomalous magnetic moment (g-2 — how much a muon's magnetism deviates from the simplest prediction) is one of physics' sharpest tests of the Standard Model. The single largest theoretical uncertainty is the hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP) — the muon's brief interaction with a cloud of quarks and gluons, which is hard to compute. This paper computes the leading HVP term with a continuum-QCD method (Dyson-Schwinger and Bethe-Salpeter equations) rather than the usual lattice simulations, obtaining about 709.7e-10 — in good agreement with recent lattice results — and quantifies a small isospin-breaking correction. What is new is the independent route: agreement between two very different frameworks makes the value more trustworthy.
story.why: Whether the muon g-2 signals new physics hinges on this exact number; a second method landing on the lattice value strengthens the picture that the older tension was a theory-input problem, not a crack in the Standard Model.

story.headline: A CRISPR enzyme programmed to shred DNA only inside cancer cells
story.summary: Two Nature papers program the CRISPR nuclease Cas12a2 to recognize a cancer-specific RNA and trigger collateral DNA destruction selectively in those cells, killing tumour cells in mouse models.
story.display_body: Many cancers are driven by mutations in tumour-suppressor genes such as TP53 (mutated in 40-50% of cancers) that are considered undruggable — the faulty protein has no pocket a drug can grip. Zeng et al., with a companion paper by Scholz et al., repurpose Cas12a2, a bacterial CRISPR nuclease that, once it recognizes a target RNA, goes on to chew up nearby DNA indiscriminately (a collateral self-destruct that bacteria use to abort viral infections). By programming Cas12a2 with a guide matching a cancer-specific transcript, the enzyme stays dormant in healthy cells but, in cells expressing the mutant RNA, triggers collateral DNA cleavage and kills them — selectively limiting tumour-cell growth in mouse models. Results are in cell and mouse models, not patients.
story.why: It reframes an undruggable mutation as a targeting signal rather than a target — turning the presence of a cancer's own faulty RNA into the trigger that destroys it, a genuinely different therapeutic logic.

story.headline: A star found whose orbit could probe the spin of the Milky Way's black hole
story.summary: The GRAVITY Collaboration reports S301, a faint main-sequence star on an 8.7-year orbit around Sgr A* with a small enough pericenter to become sensitive to the black hole's spin.
story.display_body: For decades, tracking individual stars looping around Sagittarius A* — the 4.3-million-solar-mass black hole at the Galactic centre — has let astronomers weigh it and confirm Einsteinian effects like the gravitational redshift. But those effects scale as (v/c) squared; the black hole's spin only shows up one order deeper, at (v/c) cubed, and no known star orbited fast and close enough to reach it. The GRAVITY team now reports S301, a faint (K-band magnitude 19.3) main-sequence star on an 8.7-year orbit with a small enough closest approach that, with continued monitoring, its orbit becomes sensitive to the spin — the Kerr (rotating-black-hole) correction to spacetime. This is the discovery of a suitable probe, not yet a spin measurement.
story.why: Measuring a black hole's spin from a star's orbit would be a first, and a stringent new test of general relativity in the strong-field regime right at our Galaxy's heart.
affiliations: Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics

story.headline: A model-independent hint of an anomaly in the recent expansion of the Universe
story.summary: A model-independent spline reconstruction of DESI DR2 BAO plus DES supernovae finds a localized ~3.5-sigma deviation from Planck LambdaCDM near redshift z=0.47.
story.display_body: Instead of assuming a specific dark-energy model, the authors reconstruct the Universe's expansion history directly from data — the latest DESI DR2 baryon-acoustic-oscillation measurements (a standard ruler imprinted on galaxy clustering) plus the DES Type Ia supernova compilation (standard-candle explosions) — using a flexible spline fit. Compared against the standard Planck LambdaCDM prediction, they find a localized deviation over redshift 0.3 to 0.6, peaking at about 3.5 sigma near z=0.47, and show it survives changes of method, dataset and calibration. At 3.5 sigma this is an indication, not a discovery (particle physics reserves discovery for 5 sigma), and mock tests argue it is not a reconstruction artefact.
story.why: It adds to a run of recent DESI-driven hints that dark energy may not be a constant — the kind of localized feature that, if it holds up, would point to genuinely new cosmological physics.

story.headline: The most complex sugar yet seen in interstellar space
story.summary: Astronomers detect erythrulose, a four-carbon sugar, in a molecular cloud toward the Galactic centre — the largest sugar molecule identified in interstellar space to date.
story.display_body: Sugars (monosaccharides) are core biomolecules — metabolic fuel and the backbone of DNA and RNA — and how they first arose is a central origin-of-life question, because lab experiments under early-Earth conditions make them only in trace amounts. Astronomers report detecting erythrulose, a four-carbon sugar, in a molecular cloud toward the Galactic centre — the largest sugar molecule identified in interstellar space to date, building on earlier detections of simpler two- and three-carbon species. The identification comes from matching the molecule's rotational spectral fingerprints in radio-telescope data. It suggests the chemistry that builds sugars can proceed in cold interstellar gas, before planets even form.
story.why: It pushes the inventory of complex prebiotic molecules available in space one rung up the sugar ladder, supporting the idea that some building blocks for life were delivered to young planets ready-made rather than assembled from scratch.
